history: YOLO/MediaPipe hand keypoint detection → Rep counting + form-feedback for hand rehabilitation exercises with real-time overlay
loo_notes: This hits the dual-skill sweet spot—real-time CV inference (MediaPipe/YOLO) plus meaningful engineering (rep counting, form feedback logic, UI overlay). The user need is clear and the 30-day window is realistic. One concern: form feedback quality depends heavily on the heuristics you write rather than a learned model—consider fine-tuning a lightweight pose classifier on rehab-specific examples to deepen the AI component and strengthen the reflection on what the model learns vs. what hand-coded rules miss.

history: Computer vision model trained on labeled pothole dataset → Severity classification (minor/moderate/severe) with bounding box + severity label demo
loo_notes: Solid dual-component CV plan (detection + classification) with clear real-world need and demo potential, but missing the practical engineering hook that pushes it to gold standard—no mention of inference pipeline integration, mobile/edge deployment, or how severity labels actually drive actionable outcomes for maintenance crews. The 30-day timeline is tight for annotation + training + evaluation unless you're using pre-labeled public data; clarify your data strategy.

history: Tree-LSTM (recursive neural net over parse tree) → Sentiment or NLI task vs flat sequential LSTM baseline
loo_notes: This is a pure modeling exercise with no real-world application or user need attached — you're comparing architectures on a benchmark, not solving a problem anyone actually has. Tree-LSTM is theoretically interesting but you're missing the engineering half (inference pipeline, UI, integration) and the reflection will be academic, not grounded in failure modes that matter to a stakeholder.

history: Context-aware AAC symbol predictor (LM or retrieval) → Adaptive board reordering vs frequency-baseline with click-count evaluation
loo_notes: The plan targets a genuine, high-impact user need (AAC accessibility) and proposes a measurable evaluation (click-count improvement over frequency baseline), which is strong. However, it's thin on the AI depth side: retrieval-based prediction or a lightweight LM wrapper lacks the 'train or fine-tune' signal the assignment demands, and the core challenge appears to be UI interaction logic rather than model design. 30 days is tight for collecting real user click data, labeling interactions, and training adaptive ranking—you need a concrete fallback to synthetic/offline evaluation and a clear specification of whether you're fine-tuning a pre-trained model or building a simpler learned ranker.
